query: Compare Adidas presence in third-party event coverage (World Cup, Olympics, Euros) vs in its own brand films (NMD, YEEZY, Samba, You Got This).

payload.narrative_summary:
Adidas shows up in two clearly different visual modes. In earned exposure, the brand is embedded inside third-party sports coverage: the strongest examples are the 2010 World Cup final’s Adidas Jabulani match ball and the 2024 Euro final’s Adidas-equipped on-pitch environment and kits. These moments read as infrastructural and tournament-native — Adidas is present because it supplies the game, not because the edit pauses to sell the brand.

Owned exposure is broader and much more deliberate. From the 2012 Team GB kit launch through Messi’s 2014 World Cup-era film, the 2015 Yeezy Boost design animation, the 2016 NMD launch, the 2018 Parley collaboration film, and 2024’s You Got This spots and Samba product storytelling, Adidas controls framing, pacing, and iconography. The visual grammar shifts from incidental match-context branding to hero shots, isolated product rotations, trefoil end cards, three-stripe logos, and design-detail closeups.

The balance of the corpus therefore favors owned storytelling as the richer source of brand intelligence: it reveals how Adidas wants to be seen across performance, fashion, sustainability, and motivation. Earned exposure is narrower but highly credible, because it ties Adidas to iconic competitive moments and tournament hardware. Together, the two modes show Adidas functioning both as a sports-event supplier embedded in global spectacle and as a self-authored lifestyle/performance brand with strong control over product meaning.

estimated_value.calculation_basis: Estimated at $50,000 per distinct Adidas-visible clip identified in the corpus, using 10 visually grounded clips total across earned event footage and owned brand/campaign films.
